metadata.narrative_arc: The user is locked out of an old email account tied to several active subscriptions and finds calm footing through patient, step-by-step recovery guidance.

events.description: User arrives stressed, explaining they cannot log in to an old Hotmail address they haven't touched in two years — it is the recovery address for three paid subscriptions and they have been frantically clicking 'Forgot password' with no luck.

Assistant acknowledges the stress and suggests slowing down: frantic clicking can trigger temporary lockouts and make things worse. Proposes a clear three-step path — (1) check what recovery options Microsoft has on file, (2) try the account recovery form if those are gone, (3) contact subscription services directly as a fallback — and asks the user to start with step one.

User tries the Microsoft recovery page and finds only an old phone number that is no longer active. They feel deflated and ask whether this means the account is gone forever, adding 'I knew I should have just calm steps beat frantic clicking — I've been at this for an hour and made it worse, haven't I?'

Assistant normalises the situation — losing access to old recovery contacts is extremely common — and moves to step two: walking the user carefully through Microsoft's account recovery form, explaining exactly what information to provide (previous passwords, subject lines of old emails, contacts they remember) to maximise approval chances. Reminds them that practical help builds trust faster than deep talk, so let's just work through each field together.

User completes and submits the recovery form with the assistant's guidance. They report feeling noticeably calmer than when they arrived and say 'okay, that actually felt manageable — I didn't think we'd get this far.' They ask what to do while waiting for Microsoft's response, specifically about the subscriptions.

key_moments.what_happened: When the user arrived frantic and confessed they had been clicking for an hour and may have made things worse, the assistant's suggestion to slow down and follow a numbered plan landed — the user visibly shifted from scattered urgency to a willingness to work through it one step at a time.
key_moments.why_it_matters: The user's stress was real and self-reinforcing; naming the pattern of frantic clicking and offering a concrete alternative demonstrated that calm steps beat frantic clicking — a principle that paid off immediately in the user's behaviour.
key_moments.what_changed: User stopped cycling through the same failed actions and engaged with a structured plan.

key_moments.what_happened: After the user found only an unusable old phone number and asked whether the account was gone forever, the assistant moved directly to the account recovery form and worked through each field with them — and the user submitted it successfully.
key_moments.why_it_matters: Getting the form submitted was a tangible win that rebuilt the user's confidence; it also showed that practical help builds trust faster than deep talk — echoing the principle questioned in session 6, and validating it here in a low-stakes but concrete situation.
principles_confirmed: practical help builds trust faster than deep talk
key_moments.what_changed: User moved from feeling the account was unrecoverable to having a real recovery request in flight.

expected_session_outcome.key_insight: Breaking a stressful technical spiral into numbered steps restored the user's sense of agency; calm, practical guidance proved more useful than reassurance alone, and the user ended the session with a concrete action completed rather than just a feeling of being heard.
